title: Enterprise Java Architect Assessment
description: Architect-level evaluation focusing on enterprise Java patterns, system design, performance tuning, integration architectures (JMS, Kafka, SOAP/REST), legacy modernization (Struts/EJB to Spring Boot), distributed transactions, caching strategies, and cloud-native Java deployments.

questions.text: When would you choose Apache Kafka over traditional JMS (ActiveMQ/RabbitMQ) in an enterprise Java architecture?
options: For high-throughput event streaming, event sourcing, and when message replay is required
For small-scale deployments with < 10 messages per second
When exactly-once delivery is not important
questions.explanation: Kafka excels at high-throughput persistent event storage, replayability, and stream processing. JMS is better for traditional point-to-point and pub-sub with lower throughput requirements.

questions.text: What is the primary architectural difference between stateful session beans (SFSB) and stateless session beans (SLSB) in Jakarta EE?
options: SFSB maintain conversational state across multiple client calls; SLSB are stateless and pooled, making them more scalable
SFSB are deprecated in Jakarta EE 10
questions.explanation: SFSB preserves state across calls but limits scalability. SLSB are stateless, pooled, and can handle many clients, making them the default choice for scalable enterprise apps.

questions.text: What is the 'Strangler Fig Pattern' and how is it applied to modernizing legacy Java applications (e.g., Struts/EJB to Spring Boot)?
options: Gradually replace specific functionalities with new microservices, routing traffic between legacy and new systems until full replacement
Rewrite everything from scratch in one release
Keep both systems running permanently without integration
questions.explanation: The Strangler pattern incrementally migrates legacy systems by intercepting requests and routing to new services, eventually strangling the old system. Minimizes risk and allows continuous delivery.

questions.text: In a distributed transaction across multiple microservices, why would you choose the 'Saga Pattern' over XA (Two-Phase Commit)?
options: Saga provides ACID guarantees; XA provides eventual consistency
XA requires distributed transaction coordinator and locks resources; Saga uses compensating transactions for better scalability and availability
Saga is only for NoSQL databases
questions.explanation: XA transactions lock resources and reduce availability. Saga provides eventual consistency without locks, using compensating transactions, better suited for microservices where long-lived transactions are problematic.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'Application-Level Caching' (e.g., Caffeine, Ehcache) vs 'Distributed Caching' (e.g., Redis, Hazelcast)?
options: No difference; both are the same
Application cache is local to each JVM (fast but not shared); distributed cache is shared across JVMs (slower but consistent)
Application cache requires a separate server
questions.explanation: Local caches (Caffeine) provide sub-microsecond access but each node has its own copy, causing inconsistency. Distributed caches (Redis) provide consistency across nodes with network overhead.

questions.text: How does 'Read-Through' caching differ from 'Cache-Aside' pattern?
options: Cache-Aside: app checks cache, then DB; Read-Through: cache sits between app and DB, cache loads missing data automatically
Read-Through is faster but less reliable
Cache-Aside is only for write operations
questions.explanation: Cache-Aside (lazy loading) gives app control. Read-Through delegates loading responsibility to cache library, simplifying code but reducing flexibility.

questions.text: What is the 'CQRS' pattern and when is it appropriate in enterprise Java systems?
options: Separating read and write models (different data structures/databases) for high-performance systems with different read/write scalability needs
questions.explanation: CQRS allows read and write models to scale independently. Useful when read/write ratios are extreme (e.g., 1000:1) or when read model needs denormalized views for complex queries.

questions.text: What is 'Database Sharding' and what are the common sharding strategies in enterprise Java?
options: Copying data to multiple servers for reads
Horizontal partitioning of data across multiple databases based on a shard key (hash, range, or directory-based)
questions.explanation: Sharding distributes data across databases to scale beyond single-node limits. Strategies: hash (even distribution), range (ordered data), or directory (lookup service).

questions.text: In a high-traffic enterprise Java application, what is the purpose of a 'Connection Pool' (e.g., HikariCP) and what are optimal settings?
options: To reuse database connections, reducing overhead; optimal pool size = (core_count * 2) + effective_spindle_count, typically 10-20 connections per instance
questions.explanation: Connection pooling avoids expensive connection creation. HikariCP is default in Spring Boot. Formula: pool_size = Tn * (Cm - 1) + 1. Too large causes contention; too small causes waits.

questions.text: What is the 'Outbox Pattern' and which problem does it solve in event-driven architectures?
options: Ensuring reliable event publication alongside database writes without using distributed transactions (stores events in outbox table, published by separate process)
questions.explanation: The Outbox pattern solves dual-write problem (DB write + event publish). Events stored in same transaction, then published via poller or CDC (Debezium), ensuring at-least-once delivery.

questions.text: What are the trade-offs between 'Containerization (Docker)' and 'Traditional VMs' for enterprise Java deployments?
options: Containers share host OS kernel (lightweight, fast startup) but less isolation; VMs provide full isolation (better security) with more overhead
questions.explanation: Containers share kernel, reducing overhead and startup time. VMs have stronger isolation boundaries but higher resource usage. Java container awareness (memory limits) requires -XX:+UseContainerSupport (Java 10+).

questions.text: What is 'Blue-Green Deployment' and how does it enable zero-downtime releases for Java applications?
options: Deploying half the code on Monday, half on Friday
Running two identical production environments (blue=current, green=new); switching router traffic after validation allows instant rollback
questions.explanation: Blue-green eliminates downtime by maintaining two environments. Switch traffic via load balancer. Rollback is instantaneous by switching back to blue.

questions.text: What is 'Circuit Breaker' pattern and which Java libraries implement it (e.g., Resilience4j)?
options: A pattern preventing cascading failures; states: CLOSED → OPEN → HALF-OPEN; Resilience4j, Hystrix (legacy), Spring Cloud Circuit Breaker
questions.explanation: Circuit breaker wraps external calls. OPEN fails fast, HALF-OPEN tests recovery. Resilience4j is modern replacement for Netflix Hystrix.

questions.text: What is the 'Bulkhead Pattern' and how does it isolate failures in enterprise Java?
options: Isolating resources (thread pools, connection pools) per service/tenant to prevent one failure from exhausting all resources
questions.explanation: Bulkhead partitions resources. Resilience4j provides bulkhead (semaphore or thread pool). Prevents slow downstream service from consuming all application threads.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'Java Flight Recorder (JFR)' and 'Java Mission Control (JMC)' in production troubleshooting?
options: To continuously collect low-overhead JVM telemetry (CPU, memory, locks, GC) for post-mortem or live analysis with minimal performance impact (<1%)
questions.explanation: JFR provides production-safe, always-on profiling. JMC visualizes JFR recordings. Essential for diagnosing performance issues in production without restarting JVM.

questions.text: When would you choose 'gRPC' over 'REST' for internal microservice communication in Java?
options: For low-latency, high-throughput service-to-service calls with strong contract (Protobuf) and bidirectional streaming support
questions.explanation: gRPC uses Protobuf (binary) and HTTP/2, offering lower latency, smaller payloads, multiplexing, and bidirectional streaming. REST (JSON/HTTP/1.1) is better for public APIs and browser clients.

questions.text: What is 'Service Mesh' (Istio/Linkerd) and what problems does it solve for Java microservices?
options: A dedicated infrastructure layer for service-to-service communication (observability, security, traffic management) without changing application code
questions.explanation: Service mesh offloads network concerns (retries, timeouts, mTLS, tracing, canary routing) to sidecar proxies. Allows Java developers to focus on business logic, not infrastructure.

questions.text: What is the 'Ambassador Pattern' and how is it used for legacy Java system integration?
options: A helper service that handles cross-cutting concerns (retry, auth, monitoring) on behalf of a consumer when calling external/legacy systems
questions.explanation: Ambassador pattern creates an intermediary that handles network issues, authentication, and protocol translation when connecting to legacy systems or external APIs.

questions.text: What is the difference between '@Async' in Spring and 'Reactive Programming (WebFlux)' for handling concurrency?
options: @Async uses thread pools (one thread per request); WebFlux uses event loop (few threads handling many requests) for higher concurrency with less memory
questions.explanation: @Async blocks threads (thread-per-request model). WebFlux uses non-blocking I/O with event loop, handling more concurrent connections with fewer threads, ideal for I/O-heavy workloads.

questions.text: What is the 'Sidecar Pattern' and how does it differ from 'Ambassador Pattern'?
options: Sidecar runs alongside main container (attached); Ambassador is a proxy that sits between client and service (may be separate deployment)
Sidecar is for databases; Ambassador is for HTTP
questions.explanation: Sidecar is co-located with main application (same pod in K8s). Ambassador is a proxy that handles communication to external services and can be deployed separately.

questions.text: What is 'Polyglot Persistence' and why would an enterprise Java architect choose it?
options: Using different database technologies (PostgreSQL for relational, MongoDB for documents, Redis for cache, Neo4j for graphs) within same system based on data access patterns
questions.explanation: Polyglot persistence matches database technology to workload. Relational for ACID transactions, document for flexible schemas, graph for relationships, timeseries for metrics.

questions.text: What is 'Event Sourcing' and what are its implications for audit and debugging?
options: Storing all state changes as immutable events; enables full audit trail, time travel debugging, and rebuilding state at any point in time
questions.explanation: Event Sourcing persists every change as an event. Current state derived by replaying events. Provides complete audit history and enables temporal queries.

questions.text: What is the 'Two-General's Problem' and why does it affect distributed transactions?
options: The impossibility of guaranteeing exactly-once delivery in unreliable networks; affects distributed commit protocols (2PC can block indefinitely on coordinator failure)
questions.explanation: Two Generals' Problem proves that reliable communication over unreliable networks cannot guarantee agreement. This is why distributed systems use at-least-once with idempotency or exactly-once with consensus protocols.

questions.text: What is 'Infrastructure as Code (IaC)' and which tools are common in Java enterprise environments?
options: Managing infrastructure via version-controlled definition files; Terraform, Pulumi (Java SDK), AWS CDK, Ansible
questions.explanation: IaC treats infrastructure (VMs, networks, load balancers) as code. Pulumi allows Java developers to write infrastructure in Java, leveraging type safety and IDE features.

questions.text: What is 'Garbage Collection (GC) Tuning' and when should an architect intervene in default GC settings?
options: When experiencing pause time issues (latency) or throughput degradation; selecting GC algorithm (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah) and tuning heap sizes based on application characteristics
questions.explanation: Default GC may not suit all workloads. G1GC is default for low-pause. ZGC/Shenandoah for sub-millisecond pauses. Tune heap size (-Xms/-Xmx), GC threads, and region sizes based on metrics.
